(**High-Definition Multimedia Interface**) is a brand of proprietary digital interface used to transmit high-quality video and audio signals between devices. It is commonly used to connect devices such as [televisions](/wiki/Television "Television"), [computer monitors](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor"), [projectors](/wiki/Projector "Projector"), [gaming consoles](/wiki/Gaming_console "Gaming console"), and [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer").[[2]](#cite_note-HDMIFAQ-2) HDMI supports [uncompressed video](/wiki/Uncompressed_video "Uncompressed video") and either [compressed](/wiki/Audio_compression_(data) "Audio compression (data)") or [uncompressed](/wiki/Uncompressed_audio "Uncompressed audio") [digital audio](/wiki/Digital_audio "Digital audio"), allowing a single cable to carry both signals.

**HDMI** (**High-Definition Multimedia Interface**) is a brand of proprietary digital interface used to transmit high-quality video and audio signals between devices. It is commonly used to connect devices such as [televisions](/wiki/Television "Television"), [computer monitors](/wiki/Computer_monitor "Computer monitor"), [projectors](/wiki/Projector "Projector"), [gaming consoles](/wiki/Gaming_console "Gaming console"), and [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer").[[2]](#cite_note-HDMIFAQ-2) HDMI supports [uncompressed video](/wiki/Uncompressed_video "Uncompressed video") and either [compressed](/wiki/Audio_compression_(data) "Audio compression (data)") or [uncompressed](/wiki/Uncompressed_audio "Uncompressed audio") [digital audio](/wiki/Digital_audio "Digital audio"), allowing a single cable to carry both signals.

Introduced in 2003, HDMI largely replaced older [analog video](/wiki/Analog_video "Analog video") standards such as [composite video](/wiki/Composite_video "Composite video"), [S-Video](/wiki/S-Video "S-Video"), and [VGA](/wiki/VGA_connector "VGA connector") in [consumer electronics](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics"). It was developed based on the [CEA-861](/wiki/CEA-861 "CEA-861") standard, which was also used with the earlier [Digital Visual Interface](/wiki/Digital_Visual_Interface "Digital Visual Interface") (DVI). HDMI is electrically compatible with DVI video signals, and [adapters](/wiki/Adapter "Adapter") allow interoperability between the two without signal conversion or loss of quality. Adapters and active converters are also available for connecting HDMI to other video interfaces, including the older analog formats, as well as digital formats such as [DisplayPort](/wiki/DisplayPort "DisplayPort").

HDMI has gone through multiple revisions since its introduction, with each version adding new features while maintaining backward compatibility. In addition to transmitting audio and video, HDMI also supports data transmission for features such as [Consumer Electronics Control](/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Control "Consumer Electronics Control") (CEC), which allows devices to control each other through a single [remote](/wiki/Remote_control "Remote control"), and the [HDMI Ethernet Channel](/wiki/HDMI_Ethernet_Channel "HDMI Ethernet Channel") (HEC), which enables network connectivity between compatible devices. It also supports the [Display Data Channel](/wiki/Display_Data_Channel "Display Data Channel") (DDC), used for automatic configuration between source devices and displays. Newer versions include advanced capabilities such as [3D video](/wiki/3D_video "3D video"), higher resolutions, and [expanded color spaces](/wiki/High-dynamic-range_television "High-dynamic-range television"). The [Audio Return Channel](/wiki/Audio_Return_Channel "Audio Return Channel") (ARC) and Enhanced Audio Return Channel (eARC) allow audio to be sent from a display back to an audio system over the same HDMI cable. Smaller connector types, Mini and Micro HDMI, were also introduced for use with compact devices like [camcorders](/wiki/Camcorder "Camcorder") and [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer").

As of January 2026,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit) nearly 14 billion HDMI-enabled devices have been sold worldwide, making it one of the most widely adopted [audio/video interfaces](/wiki/Audio_and_video_interfaces_and_connectors "Audio and video interfaces and connectors") in consumer electronics.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The HDMI founders were [Hitachi](/wiki/Hitachi "Hitachi"), [Panasonic](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic"), [Sanyo](/wiki/Sanyo "Sanyo") (as a Panasonic affiliate), [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips"), [Silicon Image](/wiki/Silicon_Image "Silicon Image") (now [Lattice Semiconductor](/wiki/Lattice_Semiconductor "Lattice Semiconductor")), [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), [Thomson](/wiki/Vantiva "Vantiva") (now [Vantiva](/wiki/Vantiva "Vantiva")), and [Toshiba](/wiki/Toshiba "Toshiba").[[4]](#cite_note-4) [Intel](/wiki/Intel "Intel") contributed the [HDCP](/wiki/HDCP "HDCP") copy protection system.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The new format won the support of motion picture studios [Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Studios "20th Century Studios"), [Universal](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"), [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") and [Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_(division) "Walt Disney Studios (division)"), along with content distributors [DirecTV](/wiki/DirecTV "DirecTV"), [EchoStar](/wiki/EchoStar "EchoStar") ([Dish Network](/wiki/Dish_Network "Dish Network")) and [CableLabs](/wiki/CableLabs "CableLabs").[[2]](#cite_note-HDMIFAQ-2)

The HDMI founders began development on HDMI 1.0 on April 16, 2002, with the goal of creating an AV connector that was backward-compatible with DVI.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-HDMIWGpaper-7) At the time, DVI-HDCP (DVI with HDCP) and DVI-HDTV (DVI-HDCP using the CEA-861-B video standard) were being used on HDTVs.[[7]](#cite_note-HDMIWGpaper-7)[[8]](#cite_note-HDMI2007WhitePaper-8) HDMI 1.0 was designed to improve on DVI-HDTV by using a smaller connector and adding audio capability, enhanced [Y′CBCR](/wiki/YCbCr "YCbCr") capability, and consumer electronics control functions.[[7]](#cite_note-HDMIWGpaper-7)[[8]](#cite_note-HDMI2007WhitePaper-8)

The first Authorized Testing Center (ATC), which tests HDMI products, was opened by [Silicon Image](/wiki/Silicon_Image "Silicon Image") on June 23, 2003, in California, United States.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The first ATC in Japan was opened by [Panasonic](/wiki/Panasonic "Panasonic") on May 1, 2004, in Osaka.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The first ATC in Europe was opened by [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") on May 25, 2005, in Caen, France.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The first ATC in China was opened by Silicon Image on November 21, 2005, in Shenzhen.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The first ATC in India was opened by Philips on June 12, 2008, in Bangalore.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The HDMI website contains a list of all the ATCs.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

According to In-Stat, the number of HDMI devices sold was 5 million in 2004, 17.4 million in 2005, 63 million in 2006, and 143 million in 2007.[[15]](#cite_note-HDMIIn-Stat-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) HDMI has become the [de facto standard](/wiki/De_facto_standard "De facto standard") for HDTVs, and according to In-Stat, around 90% of digital televisions in 2007 included HDMI.[[15]](#cite_note-HDMIIn-Stat-15)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) In-Stat has estimated that 229 million HDMI devices were sold in 2008.[[22]](#cite_note-22) On April 8, 2008, there were over 850 [consumer electronics](/wiki/Consumer_electronics "Consumer electronics") and PC companies that had adopted the HDMI specification (HDMI adopters).[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-HDMI2009Future-24) On January 7, 2009, HDMI Licensing, LLC announced that HDMI had reached an installed base of over 600 million HDMI devices.[[24]](#cite_note-HDMI2009Future-24) In-Stat estimated that 394 million HDMI devices would sell in 2009 and that all digital televisions by the end of 2009 would have at least one HDMI input.[[24]](#cite_note-HDMI2009Future-24)

On January 28, 2008, In-Stat reported that shipments of HDMI were expected to exceed those of DVI in 2008, driven primarily by the consumer electronics market.[[15]](#cite_note-HDMIIn-Stat-15)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2008, *[PC Magazine](/wiki/PC_Magazine "PC Magazine")* awarded a Technical Excellence Award in the Home Theater category for an "innovation that has changed the world" to the CEC portion of the HDMI specification.[[26]](#cite_note-26) Ten companies were given a Technology and Engineering Emmy Award for their development of HDMI by the [National Academy of Television Arts and Sciences](/wiki/National_Academy_of_Television_Arts_and_Sciences "National Academy of Television Arts and Sciences") on January 7, 2009.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

On October 25, 2011, the [HDMI Forum](#HDMI_Forum) was established by the HDMI founders.[[28]](#cite_note-HDMIPressRelease20111025-28)Also on the same day HDMI Licensing, LLC announced that there were over 1,100 HDMI adopters and that over 2 billion HDMI-enabled products had shipped since the launch of the HDMI standard.[[29]](#cite_note-HDMIAdopters-29)[[28]](#cite_note-HDMIPressRelease20111025-28)

On January 8, 2013, HDMI Licensing, LLC announced that there were over 1,300 HDMI adopters and that over 3 billion HDMI devices had shipped since the launch of the HDMI standard.[[30]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteJanuary2013-30)[[31]](#cite_note-BusinessHDMIJanuary2013CESPress-31) The day also marked the 10th anniversary of the release of the first HDMI specification.[[30]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteJanuary2013-30)[[31]](#cite_note-BusinessHDMIJanuary2013CESPress-31)

As of January 2021,[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit) nearly 10 billion HDMI devices had been sold.[[32]](#cite_note-ces2021pr-32)

## Specifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Specifications")]

The HDMI specification defines the protocols, signals, electrical interfaces and mechanical requirements of the standard.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): p. V The maximum pixel clock rate for HDMI 1.0 is 165 [MHz](/wiki/MHz "MHz"), which is sufficient to allow [1080p](/wiki/1080p "1080p") and [WUXGA](/wiki/WUXGA "WUXGA") (1920×1200) at 60 Hz. HDMI 1.3 increases that to 340 MHz, which allows for higher resolution (such as [WQXGA](/wiki/Graphic_display_resolutions#WQXGA "Graphic display resolutions"), 2560×1600) across a single digital link.[[34]](#cite_note-HDMI2006pr-34) An HDMI connection can either be single-link (type A/C/D) or dual-link (type B) and can have a video pixel rate of 25 MHz to 340 MHz (for a single-link connection) or 25 MHz to 680 MHz (for a dual-link connection). Video formats with pixel rates below 25 MHz (like [480i](/wiki/480i "480i") at 13.5 MHz) are transmitted over TMDS links using a pixel-repetition scheme.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §§3, 6.4

### Audio/video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Audio/video")]

HDMI uses the [Consumer Technology Association](/wiki/Consumer_Technology_Association "Consumer Technology Association")/[Electronic Industries Alliance](/wiki/Electronic_Industries_Alliance "Electronic Industries Alliance") 861 standards. HDMI 1.0 to HDMI 1.2a uses the EIA/CEA-861-B video standard, HDMI 1.3 uses the CEA-861-D video standard, and HDMI 1.4 uses the CEA-861-E video standard.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): III The CEA-861-E document defines "video formats and waveforms; colorimetry and quantization; transport of compressed and uncompressed [LPCM](/wiki/LPCM "LPCM") audio; carriage of auxiliary data; and implementations of the [Video Electronics Standards Association](/wiki/Video_Electronics_Standards_Association "Video Electronics Standards Association") (VESA) [Enhanced Extended Display Identification Data Standard](/wiki/Extended_display_identification_data#Enhanced_EDID_(E-EDID) "Extended display identification data") (E-EDID)".[[35]](#cite_note-CEA-861-E_Standards_Details-35) On July 15, 2013, the CEA announced the publication of CEA-861-F, a standard that can be used by video interfaces such as DVI, HDMI, and LVDS.[[36]](#cite_note-CEA861FpublishedJuly2013Businesswire-36) CEA-861-F adds the ability to transmit several [Ultra HD](/wiki/Ultra_HD "Ultra HD") video formats and additional color spaces.[[36]](#cite_note-CEA861FpublishedJuly2013Businesswire-36)

To ensure baseline compatibility between different HDMI sources and displays (as well as backward compatibility with the electrically compatible DVI standard) all HDMI devices must implement the [sRGB](/wiki/SRGB "SRGB") color space at 8 bits per component.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §6.2.3 Ability to use the Y′CBCR color space and higher color depths ("deep color") is optional. HDMI permits sRGB 4:4:4 [chroma sampling](/wiki/Chroma_sampling "Chroma sampling") (8–16 bits per component), [xvYCC](/wiki/XvYCC "XvYCC") 4:4:4 chroma sampling (8–16 bits per component), Y′CBCR 4:4:4 chroma sampling (8–16 bits per component), or Y′CBCR 4:2:2 chroma subsampling (8–12 bits per component). The color spaces that can be used by HDMI are [ITU-R BT.601](/wiki/ITU-R_BT.601 "ITU-R BT.601"), [ITU-R BT.709-5](/wiki/Rec._709 "Rec. 709") and [IEC 61966-2-4](/wiki/IEC_61966-2-4 "IEC 61966-2-4").[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §§6.5,6.7.2

For digital audio, if an HDMI device has audio, it is required to implement the baseline format: stereo (uncompressed) PCM. Other formats are optional, with HDMI allowing up to 8 channels of uncompressed audio at sample sizes of 16 bits, 20 bits, or 24 bits, with sample rates of 32 kHz, 44.1 kHz, 48 kHz, 88.2 kHz, 96 kHz, 176.4 kHz, or 192 kHz.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §7 HDMI also carries any [IEC 61937](/wiki/IEC_61937 "IEC 61937")-compliant compressed audio stream, such as [Dolby Digital](/wiki/Dolby_Digital "Dolby Digital") and [DTS](/wiki/Digital_Theater_System "Digital Theater System"), and up to 8 channels of one-bit [DSD](/wiki/Direct_Stream_Digital "Direct Stream Digital") audio (used on [Super Audio CDs](/wiki/Super_Audio_CD "Super Audio CD")) at rates up to four times that of Super Audio CD.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §7 With version 1.3, HDMI allows lossless compressed audio streams [Dolby TrueHD](/wiki/Dolby_TrueHD "Dolby TrueHD") and [DTS-HD Master Audio](/wiki/DTS-HD_Master_Audio "DTS-HD Master Audio").[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §7 As with the Y′CBCR video, audio capability is optional. Audio return channel (ARC) is a feature introduced in the HDMI 1.4 standard.[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37) "Return" refers to the case where the audio comes from the TV and can be sent "upstream" to the AV receiver using the HDMI cable connected to the AV receiver.[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37) An example given on the HDMI website is that a TV that directly receives a terrestrial/satellite broadcast, or has a video source built in, sends the audio "upstream" to the AV receiver.[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37)

The HDMI standard was not designed to pass [closed caption](/wiki/Closed_caption "Closed caption") data (for example, [subtitles](/wiki/Subtitles "Subtitles")) to the television for decoding.[[38]](#cite_note-38) As such, any closed caption stream must be decoded and included as an image in the video stream(s) prior to transmission over an HDMI cable to appear on the DTV. This limits the caption style (even for digital captions) to only that decoded at the source prior to HDMI transmission. This also prevents closed captions when transmission over HDMI is required for upconversion. For example, a [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") player that sends an upscaled 720p/1080i format via HDMI to an [HDTV](/wiki/HDTV "HDTV") has no way to pass [Closed Captioning](/wiki/Closed_Captioning "Closed Captioning") data so that the HDTV can decode it, as there is [no line 21 VBI](/wiki/Closed_captioning#HDTV_interoperability_issues "Closed captioning") in that format.

HDMI has three physically separate communication channels, which are the [VESA](/wiki/VESA "VESA") DDC, TMDS and the optional CEC.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §8.1 HDMI 1.4 added ARC and HEC.[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37)[[39]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteHEC-39)

#### Display Data Channel (DDC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Display Data Channel (DDC)")]

Main article: [Display Data Channel](/wiki/Display_Data_Channel "Display Data Channel")

The [Display Data Channel](/wiki/Display_Data_Channel "Display Data Channel") (DDC) is a VESA standard communications channel based on the [I2C](/wiki/I2C "I2C") bus specification. HDMI specifically requires the device implement the [Enhanced Display Data Channel](/wiki/Display_Data_Channel#Enhanced_Display_Data_Channel_(E-DDC) "Display Data Channel") (E-DDC), which is used by the HDMI source device to read the [E-EDID](/wiki/Extended_display_identification_data#Enhanced_EDID_(E-EDID) "Extended display identification data") data from the HDMI sink device to learn what audio/video formats it can take.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §§8.1, CEC-1.2–CEC-1.3 HDMI requires that the E-DDC implement I2C standard mode speed (100 [kbit/s](/wiki/Kbit/s "Kbit/s")) and allows it to optionally implement fast mode speed (400 kbit/s).[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.2.8

I2C address 0x74 on the DDC channel is actively used for [High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection](/wiki/High-bandwidth_Digital_Content_Protection "High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection") (HDCP).

#### Transition-minimized differential signaling (TMDS)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Transition-minimized differential signaling (TMDS)")]

[Transition-minimized differential signaling](/wiki/Transition-minimized_differential_signaling "Transition-minimized differential signaling") (TMDS) on HDMI interleaves video, audio and auxiliary data using three different packet types, called the video data period, the data island period and the control period. During the video data period, the pixels of an active video line are transmitted. During the data island period (which occurs during the horizontal and vertical blanking intervals), audio and auxiliary data are transmitted within a series of packets. The control period occurs between video and data island periods.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §5.1.2

Both HDMI and DVI use TMDS to send 10-bit characters that are encoded using [8b/10b encoding](/wiki/8b/10b_encoding "8b/10b encoding") that differs from the original IBM form for the video data period and 2b/10b encoding for the control period. HDMI adds the ability to send audio and auxiliary data using 4b/10b encoding for the data island period. Each data island period is 32 pixels in size and contains a 32-bit packet header, which includes 8 bits of [BCH](/wiki/BCH_code "BCH code") ECC parity data for [error correction](/wiki/Error_detection_and_correction#Error-correcting_code "Error detection and correction") and describes the contents of the packet. Each packet contains four subpackets, and each subpacket is 64 bits in size, including 8 bits of BCH ECC parity data, allowing for each packet to carry up to 224 bits of audio data. Each data island period can contain up to 18 packets. Seven of the 15 packet types described in the HDMI 1.3a specifications deal with audio data, while the other 8 types deal with auxiliary data. Among these are the general control packet and the gamut metadata packet. The general control packet carries information on AVMUTE (which mutes the audio during changes that may cause audio noise) and [color depth](/wiki/Color_depth "Color depth") (which sends the bit depth of the current video stream and is required for [deep color](/wiki/Deep_color "Deep color")). The gamut metadata packet carries information on the [color space](/wiki/Color_space "Color space") being used for the current video stream and is required for xvYCC.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §§5.2–5.3,6.5.3,6.7.2,6.7.3

#### Consumer Electronics Control (CEC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Consumer Electronics Control (CEC)")]

Main article: [Consumer Electronics Control](/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Control "Consumer Electronics Control")

Consumer Electronics Control (CEC) is an HDMI feature designed to allow the user to command and control up to 15 CEC-enabled devices, that are connected through HDMI,[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) by using only one of their remote controls (for example by controlling a [television set](/wiki/Television_set "Television set"), [set-top box](/wiki/Set-top_box "Set-top box"), and [DVD player](/wiki/DVD_player "DVD player") using only the remote control of the TV). CEC also allows for individual CEC-enabled devices to command and control each other without user intervention.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §CEC-3.1

It is a one-wire bidirectional [serial](/wiki/Serial_communications "Serial communications") bus that is based on the [CENELEC](/wiki/CENELEC "CENELEC") standard [AV.link](/wiki/AV.link "AV.link") protocol to perform [remote control](/wiki/Remote_control "Remote control") functions.[[42]](#cite_note-42) CEC wiring is mandatory, although implementation of CEC in a product is optional.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §8.1 It was defined in HDMI Specification 1.0 and updated in HDMI 1.2, HDMI 1.2a and HDMI 1.3a (which added timer and audio commands to the bus).[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §§CEC-1.2, CEC-1.3, CEC-3.1, CEC-5 USB to CEC adapters exist that allow a computer to control CEC-enabled devices.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46)

#### HDMI Ethernet and Audio Return Channel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: HDMI Ethernet and Audio Return Channel")]

Introduced in HDMI 1.4, HDMI Ethernet and Audio Return Channel (HEAC) adds a high-speed bidirectional data communication link (HEC) and the ability to send audio data upstream to the source device (ARC). HEAC utilizes two lines from the connector: the previously unused *Reserved* pin (called HEAC+) and the *Hot Plug Detect* pin (called HEAC−).[[47]](#cite_note-HDMI1.4a-47): §HEAC-2.1 If only ARC transmission is required, a [single mode signal](/wiki/Single-ended_signaling "Single-ended signaling") using the HEAC+ line can be used, otherwise, HEC is transmitted as a [differential signal](/wiki/Differential_signal "Differential signal") over the pair of lines, and ARC as a common mode component of the pair.[[47]](#cite_note-HDMI1.4a-47): §HEAC-2.2

##### Audio Return Channel (ARC) and Enhanced Audio Return Channel (eARC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Audio Return Channel (ARC) and Enhanced Audio Return Channel (eARC)")]

ARC (Audio Return Channel) is an audio link introduced in 2009 with the HDMI 1.4 standard meant to replace other cables between the TV and the A/V receiver or speaker system.[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37) This direction is used when the TV is the one that generates or receives the video stream instead of the other equipment.[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37) A typical case is the running of an app on a [smart TV](/wiki/Smart_TV "Smart TV") such as [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix"), but reproduction of audio is handled by the other equipment.[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37) Without ARC, the audio output from the TV must be routed by another cable, typically [TOSLink](/wiki/TOSLINK "TOSLINK") or [RCA](/wiki/RCA_connector "RCA connector"), into the speaker system.[[48]](#cite_note-48) ARC supports stereo [PCM](/wiki/PCM "PCM") and compressed codecs [Dolby Digital](/wiki/Dolby_Digital "Dolby Digital"), [Dolby Digital Plus](/wiki/Dolby_Digital_Plus "Dolby Digital Plus") and [DTS](/wiki/DTS,_Inc. "DTS, Inc.") up to 5.1 channels, with [Dolby Atmos](/wiki/Dolby_Atmos "Dolby Atmos") metadata in Dolby codecs.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

eARC (Enhanced Audio Return Channel) was introduced in 2017 with the HDMI 2.1 standard. eARC has higher bandwidth (37 Mbps) and adds support for uncompressed surround sound, [Dolby TrueHD](/wiki/Dolby_TrueHD "Dolby TrueHD") and [DTS-HD Master Audio](/wiki/DTS-HD_Master_Audio "DTS-HD Master Audio") passthrough with support for up to 32 channels. eARC requires an "Ultra High Speed", "Premium High Speed with Ethernet", or "High Speed with Ethernet" HDMI cable.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)

##### HDMI Ethernet Channel (HEC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: HDMI Ethernet Channel (HEC)")]

HDMI Ethernet Channel technology consolidates video, audio, and data streams into a single HDMI cable, and the HEC feature enables IP-based applications over HDMI and provides a bidirectional Ethernet communication at 100 [Mbit/s](/wiki/Mbit/s "Mbit/s").[[39]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteHEC-39) The [physical layer](/wiki/Ethernet_physical_layer "Ethernet physical layer") of the Ethernet implementation uses a [hybrid](/wiki/Telephone_hybrid "Telephone hybrid") to simultaneously send and receive attenuated [100BASE-TX](/wiki/100BASE-TX "100BASE-TX")-type signals through a single [twisted pair](/wiki/Twisted_pair "Twisted pair").[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)

### Compatibility with DVI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Compatibility with DVI")]

[![An adapter with a DVI receptacle connector to HDMI plug connector.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/DVI-HDMI-Adapter.jpg/250px-DVI-HDMI-Adapter.jpg)](/wiki/File:DVI-HDMI-Adapter.jpg)

An adapter with HDMI (male, right) and DVI (female, left) connectors

[![An adapter with an HDMI receptacle connector to DVI plug connector with a close up of the HDMI connector.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Adapter_dvi_hdmi_S7302224_wp.jpg/250px-Adapter_dvi_hdmi_S7302224_wp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adapter_dvi_hdmi_S7302224_wp.jpg)

An adapter with DVI (male, rear, not visible) and HDMI (female, front) connectors

HDMI is [backward compatible](/wiki/Backward_compatible "Backward compatible") with single-link [Digital Visual Interface](/wiki/Digital_Visual_Interface "Digital Visual Interface") digital video (DVI-D or DVI-I, but not DVI-A or dual-link DVI). No signal conversion is required when an adapter or asymmetric cable is used, so there is no loss of video quality.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): appx. C

From a user's perspective, an HDMI display can be driven by a single-link DVI-D source, since HDMI and DVI-D define an overlapping minimum set of allowed resolutions and frame-buffer formats to ensure a basic level of interoperability. In the reverse case, a DVI-D monitor has the same level of basic interoperability unless content protection with [High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection](/wiki/High-bandwidth_Digital_Content_Protection "High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection") (HDCP) interferes—or the HDMI color encoding is in component color space [Y′CBCR](/wiki/YCbCr "YCbCr") instead of [RGB](/wiki/RGB "RGB"), which is not possible in DVI. An HDMI source, such as a [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray") player, may require an HDCP-compliant display, and refuse to output HDCP-protected content to a non-compliant display.[[54]](#cite_note-54) A further complication is that there is a small amount of display equipment, such as some high-end home theater projectors, designed with HDMI inputs but not HDCP-compliant.

Any DVI-to-HDMI adapter can function as an HDMI-to-DVI adapter (and vice versa).[[55]](#cite_note-55) Typically, the only limitation is the [gender of the adapter's connectors](/wiki/Gender_of_connectors_and_fasteners "Gender of connectors and fasteners") and the gender of the cables and sockets it is used with.

Features specific to HDMI, such as remote-control and audio transport, are not available in devices that use legacy DVI-D signalling. However, many devices output HDMI over a DVI connector (e.g., [ATI 3000-series](/wiki/Radeon_R600 "Radeon R600") and [NVIDIA GTX 200-series](/wiki/GeForce_200_series "GeForce 200 series") video cards),[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): appx. C[[56]](#cite_note-56) and some multimedia displays may accept HDMI (including audio) over a DVI input. Exact capabilities beyond basic compatibility vary. Adapters are generally bi-directional.

### Content protection (HDCP)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Content protection (HDCP)")]

Main article: [High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection](/wiki/High-bandwidth_Digital_Content_Protection "High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection")

[High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection](/wiki/High-bandwidth_Digital_Content_Protection "High-bandwidth Digital Content Protection") (HDCP) is a newer form of [digital rights management](/wiki/Digital_rights_management "Digital rights management") (DRM). Intel created the original technology to make sure that digital content followed the guidelines set by the Digital Content Protection group.

HDMI can use HDCP to encrypt the signal if required by the source device. [Content Scramble System](/wiki/Content_Scramble_System "Content Scramble System") (CSS), [Content Protection for Recordable Media](/wiki/Content_Protection_for_Recordable_Media "Content Protection for Recordable Media") (CPRM) and [Advanced Access Content System](/wiki/Advanced_Access_Content_System "Advanced Access Content System") (AACS) require the use of HDCP on HDMI when playing back encrypted [DVD Video](/wiki/DVD_Video "DVD Video"), [DVD Audio](/wiki/DVD_Audio "DVD Audio"), [HD DVD](/wiki/HD_DVD "HD DVD") and [Blu-ray Discs](/wiki/Blu-ray_Discs "Blu-ray Discs"). The [HDCP repeater bit](/wiki/HDCP_repeater_bit "HDCP repeater bit") controls the authentication and switching/distribution of an HDMI signal. According to HDCP Specification 1.2 (beginning with HDMI CTS 1.3a), any system that implements HDCP must do so in a fully compliant manner. HDCP testing that was previously only a requirement for optional tests such as the "Simplay HD" testing program is now part of the requirements for HDMI compliance.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §9.2[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-HDMI20072-58) HDCP accommodates up to 127 connected devices with up to 7 levels, using a combination of sources, sinks and repeaters.[[59]](#cite_note-HDCPSummary-59) A simple example of this is several HDMI devices connected to an HDMI AV receiver that is connected to an HDMI display.[[59]](#cite_note-HDCPSummary-59)

Devices called HDCP strippers can remove the HDCP information from the video signal so the video can play on non-HDCP-compliant displays,[[60]](#cite_note-60) though a [fair use](/wiki/Fair_use "Fair use") and [non-disclosure](/wiki/Non-disclosure "Non-disclosure") form must usually be signed with a registering agency before use.

### Connectors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Connectors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/HDMI_Connector_Types.png/500px-HDMI_Connector_Types.png)](/wiki/File:HDMI_Connector_Types.png)

Connector types for HDMI

[![A close up image of the end three HDMI plugs: type D, type C and type A.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Mini_micro_HDMI_Stecker_by_NicoJenner.jpg/250px-Mini_micro_HDMI_Stecker_by_NicoJenner.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mini_micro_HDMI_Stecker_by_NicoJenner.jpg)

HDMI connector plugs (male): type D (Micro), type C (Mini), and type A

[![An HDMI type A receptacle connector on a device with the words HDMI IN below it.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/3f/HDMI.socket.png)](/wiki/File:HDMI.socket.png)

HDMI type A receptacle

There are five HDMI connector types. Type A/B are defined in the HDMI 1.0 specification, type C is defined in the HDMI 1.3 specification, and type D/E are defined in the HDMI 1.4 specification.

Type A; Standard
:   The plug (male) connector outside dimensions are 13.9 mm × 4.45 mm, and the receptacle (female) connector inside dimensions are 14 mm × 4.55 mm.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.1.9.2 There are 19 pins, with bandwidth to carry all [SDTV](/wiki/SDTV "SDTV"), [EDTV](/wiki/EDTV "EDTV"), [HDTV](/wiki/HDTV "HDTV"), [UHD](/wiki/Ultra-high-definition_television "Ultra-high-definition television"), and 4K modes.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §6.3 It is electrically compatible with [single-link DVI-D](/wiki/DVI-D#Single-link_DVI "DVI-D").[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.1.3

Type B; Dual-link
:   This connector is 21.2 mm × 4.45 mm and has 29 pins, carrying six differential pairs instead of three, for use with very high-resolution displays such as [WQUXGA](/wiki/Graphic_display_resolutions#WQUXGA "Graphic display resolutions") (3840×2400). It is electrically compatible with [dual-link DVI-D](/wiki/DVI-D#Dual-link_DVI "DVI-D").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] With the introduction of HDMI 1.3, the maximum bandwidth of single-link HDMI exceeded that of dual-link DVI-D. As of HDMI 1.4, the pixel clock rate crossover frequency from single to dual-link has not been defined.[[47]](#cite_note-HDMI1.4a-47): §§4.1.3,4.1.9.4

Type C; Mini
:   This Mini connector is smaller than the type A plug, measuring 10.42 mm × 2.42 mm but has the same 19-pin configuration.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §§4.1.9.4,4.1.9.6 It is intended for portable devices.[[2]](#cite_note-HDMIFAQ-2)[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.1.1[[61]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14FAQ-61) The differences are that all positive signals of the differential pairs are swapped with their corresponding shield, the DDC/CEC Ground is assigned to pin 13 instead of pin 17, the CEC is assigned to pin 14 instead of pin 13, and the reserved pin is 17 instead of pin 14.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.1.10.5 The type C Mini connector can be connected to a type A connector using a type A-to-type C cable.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.1.1[[61]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14FAQ-61)

Type D; Micro
:   [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/MicroHDMI_Rpi4.jpg/250px-MicroHDMI_Rpi4.jpg)](/wiki/File:MicroHDMI_Rpi4.jpg)

    Micro HDMI receptacle

    This Micro connector shrinks the connector size to something resembling a [micro-USB](/wiki/USB#Mini_and_Micro "USB") connector,[[61]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14FAQ-61)[[62]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14PressRelease-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) measuring only 5.83 mm × 2.20 mm[[64]](#cite_note-HDMI1.4-64): 36, fig. 4.1.9.8 For comparison, a micro-USB connector is 6.85 mm × 1.8 mm and a USB type-A connector is 11.5 mm × 4.5 mm. It keeps the standard 19 pins of types A and C, but the pin assignment is different from both.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Type E; Automotive
:   The Automotive Connection System has a locking tab to keep the cable from vibrating loose and a shell to help prevent moisture and dirt from corroding the pins.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The [HDMI alternate mode](#HDMI_Alternate_Mode_for_USB_Type-C) lets a user connect the reversible [USB-C](/wiki/USB-C "USB-C") connector with the HDMI source devices (mobile, tablet, laptop). This cable connects to video display/sink devices using any of the native HDMI connectors. This is an HDMI cable, in this case a USB-C to HDMI cable.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

### Cables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Cables")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/HDMI-Kabel.jpg/250px-HDMI-Kabel.jpg)](/wiki/File:HDMI-Kabel.jpg)

A standard HDMI cable

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/HDMI_wires.jpg/250px-HDMI_wires.jpg)](/wiki/File:HDMI_wires.jpg)

HDMI pins in connector exposed

An HDMI cable is composed of four shielded [twisted pairs](/wiki/Twisted_pair "Twisted pair"), with a [characteristic impedance](/wiki/Characteristic_impedance "Characteristic impedance") of 100 [Ω](/wiki/Ohm "Ohm") (±15%), plus seven separate conductors. HDMI cables with Ethernet differ in that three of the separate conductors instead form an additional shielded twisted pair (with the CEC/DDC ground as a shield).[[47]](#cite_note-HDMI1.4a-47): §HEAC-2.9

Although no maximum length for an HDMI cable is specified, signal [attenuation](/wiki/Attenuation "Attenuation") (dependent on the cable's construction quality and conducting materials) limits usable lengths in practice[[69]](#cite_note-HDMISVarticle-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) and certification is difficult to achieve for lengths beyond 13 m.[[71]](#cite_note-Blue_Jeans_Cable's_article-71) HDMI 1.3 defines two cable categories: Category 1-certified cables, which have been tested at 74.25 MHz (which would include resolutions such as 720p60 and 1080i60), and Category 2-certified cables, which have been tested at 340 MHz (which would include resolutions such as 1080p60 and 4K30).[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.2.6[[62]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14PressRelease-62)[[72]](#cite_note-HDMI4K-72) Category 1 HDMI cables are marketed as "Standard" and Category 2 HDMI cables as "High Speed".[[2]](#cite_note-HDMIFAQ-2) This labeling guideline for HDMI cables went into effect on October 17, 2008.[[73]](#cite_note-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) Category 1 and 2 cables can either meet the required parameter specifications for inter-pair skew, far-end crosstalk, attenuation and differential impedance, or they can meet the required non-equalized/equalized eye diagram requirements.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.2.6 A cable of about 5 meters (16 feet) can be manufactured to Category 1 specifications easily and inexpensively by using 28 [AWG](/wiki/AWG "AWG") (0.081 mm²) conductors.[[69]](#cite_note-HDMISVarticle-69) With better quality construction and materials, including 24 AWG (0.205 mm²) conductors, an HDMI cable can reach lengths of up to 15 meters (49 feet).[[69]](#cite_note-HDMISVarticle-69) Many HDMI cables under 5 meters in length that were made before the HDMI 1.3 specification can work as Category 2 cables, but only Category 2-tested cables are guaranteed to work for Category 2 purposes.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

HDMI cables are certified to guarantee a certain level of performance at an Authorized Testing Center (ATC). As of the HDMI 2.2 specification, the following certifications are defined for HDMI cables in consumer applications:[[76]](#cite_note-76)

HDMI cable certifications

| Certification name | Guaranteed bit rate | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Standard HDMI Cable | 2.2275 Gbit/s | Tested up to 74.25 MHz TMDS (approximately 1280 × 720 at 60 Hz or 1920 × 1080 at 30 Hz). The ARC and inline Ethernet features require the "with Ethernet" type. |
| Standard HDMI Cable with Ethernet |
| High Speed HDMI Cable | 10.2 Gbit/s | Tested up to 340 MHz TMDS (approximately 1920 × 1080 at 144 Hz or 2560 × 1440 at 75 Hz). The ARC and inline Ethernet features require the "with Ethernet" type. |
| High Speed HDMI Cable with Ethernet |
| Premium High Speed HDMI cable | 18.0 Gbit/s | Tested up to 600 MHz TMDS (approximately 1920 × 1080 at 240 Hz, 2560 × 1440 at 144 Hz, or 3840 × 2160 at 60 Hz). The ARC and inline Ethernet features require the "with Ethernet" type. |
| Premium High Speed HDMI Cable with Ethernet |
| Ultra High Speed HDMI cable | 48.0 Gbit/s | Tested at FRL 48 Gbit/s (approximately 3840 × 2160 at 144 Hz with HDR). |
| Ultra96 HDMI cable | 96.0 Gbit/s | Tested at FRL 96 Gbit/s (approximately 3840 × 2160 at 288 Hz with HDR). |

Separate certifications also exist for "automotive" Standard and High Speed HDMI cables, which use a different connector with a latching mechanism.

### Extenders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Extenders")]

Long cables can cause instability of [HDCP](/wiki/HDCP "HDCP") and blinking on the screen due to the weakened [DDC](/wiki/Display_Data_Channel "Display Data Channel") signal that HDCP requires. An extender can be used to address this instability.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] An HDMI extender is a single device (or pair of devices) powered with an external power source or from the 5 VDC from the HDMI source.[[77]](#cite_note-HDMILongCableLengths-77)[[78]](#cite_note-XCAT-250-78)[[79]](#cite_note-HDMIFiberExtender-79)

* Standard HDMI cables
  + Active HDMI cables use electronics within the cable to boost the signal and allow for HDMI cables of up to 30 meters (98 feet).[[77]](#cite_note-HDMILongCableLengths-77)
  + Several companies offer [amplifiers](/wiki/Amplifier "Amplifier"), [equalizers](/wiki/Equalization_(communications) "Equalization (communications)"), and [repeaters](/wiki/Repeater "Repeater") that can string several standard HDMI cables together.
* Ethernet cables ([Category 5](/wiki/Category_5_cable "Category 5 cable")/[Category 6 cable](/wiki/Category_6_cable "Category 6 cable"))
  + A single cable provides 4 differential pairs. As a result, with extenders based on a single cable, HDCP DDC signals must be multiplexed with TMDS video signals.[[80]](#cite_note-HDCPLicense-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81) There is a standard for AV over single-Ethernet-cable, [HDBaseT](/wiki/HDBaseT "HDBaseT"), which is used by some extenders as the on-wire format. HDBaseT has a maximum reach of 100 meters.
  + HDMI extenders that are based on dual [Category 5](/wiki/Category_5_cable "Category 5 cable")/[Category 6 cable](/wiki/Category_6_cable "Category 6 cable") can extend HDMI to 250 meters (820 feet).[[78]](#cite_note-XCAT-250-78)
* Optical fiber: extenders based on [optical fiber](/wiki/Optical_fiber "Optical fiber") can extend HDMI to 300 meters (980 feet).[[79]](#cite_note-HDMIFiberExtender-79)
* [AV-over-IP](/w/index.php?title=AV-over-IP&action=edit&redlink=1 "AV-over-IP (page does not exist)")
  + [SDVoE](/wiki/SDVoE "SDVoE") is a libre protocol-suite for transferring real-time multimedia (video, audio, etc.) over an [IP](/wiki/Internet_Protocol "Internet Protocol") data network, via [IP multicasting](/wiki/IP_multicast "IP multicast"). Several manufacturers sell transmitters/receivers with HDMI ports. The nature of data networks means that there's no inherent limit on the achievable transmission range, and the underlying physical medium (copper, fibre, radio) is abstracted away. Transferring [HD](/wiki/High-definition_video "High-definition video") video requires high network capacity of 10Gbps. Transmitters and receivers are associated via control software, enabling versatile configurable AV-distribution; 1-to-1, 1-to-many, many-to-1.

### Licensing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Licensing")]

The HDMI specification is not an open standard; manufacturers need to be licensed by HDMI LA in order to implement HDMI in any product or component. Companies that are licensed by HDMI LA are known as HDMI Adopters.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

#### HDMI adopters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: HDMI adopters")]

While earlier versions of HDMI specs are available to the public for download, only adopters have access to the latest standards (HDMI 1.4b/2.1). Only adopters have access to the compliance test specification (CTS) that is used for compliance and certification. Compliance testing is required before any HDMI product can be legally sold.

* Adopters have IP rights under Adopter Agreement.
* Adopters receive the right to use HDMI logos and trademarks on their products and marketing materials.
* Adopters are listed on the HDMI website.
* Products from adopters are listed and marketed in the official HDMI product finder database.
* Adopters receive more exposure through combined marketing, such as the annual HDMI Developers Conference and technology seminars.

#### HDMI fee structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: HDMI fee structure")]

There are two annual fee structures associated with being an HDMI adopter:

* High-volume (more than 10,000 units) HDMI Adopter Agreement – [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")10,000 per year.[[83]](#cite_note-hdmiterms-83)
* Low-volume (10,000 units or fewer) HDMI Adopter Agreement – [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")5,000 plus a flat [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1 per unit administration fee.[[83]](#cite_note-hdmiterms-83)

The annual fee is due upon the execution of the Adopter Agreement, and must be paid on the anniversary of this date each year thereafter.

The royalty fee structure is the same for all volumes. The following variable per-unit royalty is device-based and not dependent on number of ports, chips or connectors:

* [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")0.15 – for each end-user licensed product[[83]](#cite_note-hdmiterms-83)
* [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")0.05 – if the HDMI logo is used on the product and promotional material, the per-unit fee drops from [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")0.15 to [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")0.05.[[83]](#cite_note-hdmiterms-83)
* [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")0.04 – if HDCP is implemented and HDMI logo is used, the per-unit fee drops from [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")0.05 to [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")0.04.[[83]](#cite_note-hdmiterms-83)

Use of HDMI logo requires compliance testing. Adopters need to license HDCP separately.

The HDMI royalty is only payable on licensed products that will be sold on a stand-alone basis (i.e., that are not incorporated into another licensed product that is subject to an HDMI royalty). For example, if a cable or IC is sold to an adopter who then includes it in a television subject to a royalty, then the cable or IC maker would not pay a royalty, and the television manufacturer would pay the royalty on the final product. If the cable is sold directly to consumers, then the cable would be subject to a royalty.[[83]](#cite_note-hdmiterms-83)

## Versions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Versions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/550_S._Winchester_Blvd.jpg/250px-550_S._Winchester_Blvd.jpg)](/wiki/File:550_S._Winchester_Blvd.jpg)

HDMI Licensing headquarters in [San Jose, California](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California") (in [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley"))

HDMI devices and cables are designed based on the HDMI Specification, a document published by HDMI Licensing (through version 1.4b) or the HDMI Forum (from version 2.0 onward). The HDMI Specification defines the minimum baseline requirements that all HDMI devices must adhere to for interoperability, as well as a large set of optional features that HDMI devices may support. The specification is periodically updated to add clarifications or define new capabilities that HDMI devices may implement. Each new version of the specification expands the list of possible features, but does not mandate support for new features in all devices or establish any "classes" of HDMI products which must support certain capabilities. Version numbers do not refer to classes or tiers of products with certain levels of feature support, and as such, HDMI specification "version numbers" are not a method of describing support for specific features or describing the capabilities of an HDMI device or cable.[[84]](#cite_note-hdmi_atlug-84)[[85]](#cite_note-bjc_cable_compliance-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)

In 2009, HDMI Licensing banned the use of "version numbers" in labeling HDMI products.[[87]](#cite_note-87) Instead, HDMI devices should explicitly declare which features and capabilities they support. For HDMI cables, a speed rating system was established since feature support is not dependent on the cable (apart from inline Ethernet and ARC); the cable only affects the maximum possible speed of the connection.[[85]](#cite_note-bjc_cable_compliance-85) HDMI cables should be labeled with the appropriate [speed certification](#Cables) (i.e. Standard Speed, High Speed, or Ultra High Speed), not a "version number".[[84]](#cite_note-hdmi_atlug-84)

### Version 1.0

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Version 1.0")]

HDMI 1.0 was released on December 9, 2002, and is a single-cable digital audio/video connector interface. The link architecture is based on DVI, using exactly the same video transmission format but sending audio and other auxiliary data during the blanking intervals of the video stream. HDMI 1.0 allows a maximum TMDS clock of 165 MHz (4.95 Gbit/s bandwidth per link), the same as DVI. It defines two connectors called type A and type B, with pinouts based on the Single-Link DVI-D and Dual-Link DVI-D connectors respectively, though the type B connector was never used in any commercial products. HDMI 1.0 uses TMDS encoding for video transmission, giving it 3.96 Gbit/s of video bandwidth (1920 × 1080 or 1920 × 1200 at 60 Hz) and 8-channel LPCM/192 [kHz](/wiki/KHz "KHz")/24-bit audio. HDMI 1.0 requires support for RGB video, with optional support for Y′CBCR 4:4:4 and 4:2:2 (mandatory if the device has support for Y′CBCR on other interfaces). Color depth of 10 bpc (30 bit/px) or 12 bpc (36 bit/px) is allowed when using 4:2:2 subsampling, but only 8 bpc (24 bit/px) color depth is permitted when using RGB or Y′CBCR 4:4:4. Only the [Rec. 601](/wiki/Rec._601 "Rec. 601") and [Rec. 709](/wiki/Rec._709 "Rec. 709") color spaces are supported. HDMI 1.0 allows only specific pre-defined video formats, including all the formats defined in EIA/CEA-861-B and some additional formats listed in the HDMI Specification itself. All HDMI sources/sinks must also be capable of sending/receiving native Single-Link DVI video and be fully compliant with the DVI Specification.[[88]](#cite_note-HDMI1.0-88)

### Version 1.1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Version 1.1")]

HDMI 1.1 was released on May 20, 2004, and added support for [DVD-Audio](/wiki/DVD-Audio "DVD-Audio").

### Version 1.2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Version 1.2")]

HDMI 1.2 was released on August 8, 2005, and added the option of One Bit Audio, used on [Super Audio CDs](/wiki/Super_Audio_CD "Super Audio CD"), at up to 8 channels. To make HDMI more suitable for use on PC devices, version 1.2 also removed the requirement that only explicitly supported formats be used. It added the ability for manufacturers to create vendor-specific formats, allowing any arbitrary resolution and refresh rate rather than being limited to a pre-defined list of supported formats. In addition, it added explicit support for several new formats including 720p at 100 and 120 Hz and relaxed the pixel format support requirements so that sources with only native RGB output (PC sources) would not be required to support Y′CBCR output.[[89]](#cite_note-HDMI1.2a-89): §6.2.3

**HDMI 1.2a** was released on December 14, 2005 and fully specifies [Consumer Electronic Control](/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Control "Consumer Electronics Control") (CEC) features, command sets and CEC compliance tests.[[89]](#cite_note-HDMI1.2a-89)

### Version 1.3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Version 1.3")]

HDMI 1.3 was released on June 22, 2006, and increased the maximum TMDS clock to 340 [MHz](/wiki/MHz "MHz") (10.2 Gbit/s).[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33)[[34]](#cite_note-HDMI2006pr-34)[[90]](#cite_note-90) Like previous versions, it uses TMDS encoding, giving it a maximum video bandwidth of 8.16 Gbit/s (sufficient for 1920 × 1080 at 144 Hz or 2560 × 1440 at 75 Hz). It added support for 10 bpc, 12 bpc, and 16 bpc color depth (30, 36, and 48 bit/px), called [deep color](/wiki/Deep_color "Deep color"). It also added support for the [xvYCC](/wiki/XvYCC "XvYCC") color space, in addition to the ITU-R BT.601 and BT.709 color spaces supported by previous versions, and added the ability to carry metadata defining color gamut boundaries. It also optionally allows output of [Dolby TrueHD](/wiki/Dolby_TrueHD "Dolby TrueHD") and [DTS-HD Master Audio](/wiki/DTS-HD_Master_Audio "DTS-HD Master Audio") streams for external decoding by AV receivers.[[91]](#cite_note-91) It incorporates automatic audio syncing ([audio video sync](/wiki/Audio_video_sync "Audio video sync")) capability.[[34]](#cite_note-HDMI2006pr-34) It defined cable Categories 1 and 2, with Category 1 cable being tested up to 74.25 MHz and Category 2 being tested up to 340 MHz.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.2.6 It also added the new HDMI type C "Mini" connector for portable devices.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §4.1.1[[92]](#cite_note-92)

**HDMI 1.3a** was released on November 10, 2006, and had cable and sink modifications for HDMI type C, source termination recommendations, and removed undershoot and maximum rise/fall time limits. It also changed CEC capacitance limits, and CEC commands for timer control were brought back in an altered form, with audio control commands added. It also added the optional ability to stream SACD in its bitstream DST format rather than uncompressed raw DSD.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33) HDMI 1.3a is available to download free of charge, after registration.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

### Version 1.4

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Version 1.4")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/HDMI_1.4_with_Audio_Return_Channel.png/250px-HDMI_1.4_with_Audio_Return_Channel.png)](/wiki/File:HDMI_1.4_with_Audio_Return_Channel.png)

HDMI 1.4 with audio return channel

HDMI 1.4 was released on June 5, 2009, and first came to market after Q2 of 2009.[[62]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14PressRelease-62)[[94]](#cite_note-SiliconImageHDMIVersion14-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95) Retaining the bandwidth of the previous version, HDMI 1.4 defined standardized timings to use for 4096 × 2160 at 24 Hz, 3840 × 2160 at 24, 25, and 30 Hz, and added explicit support for 1920 × 1080 at 120 Hz with CTA-861 timings.[[64]](#cite_note-HDMI1.4-64): §6.3.2 It also added an HDMI Ethernet Channel (HEC) that accommodates a 100 Mbit/s [Ethernet](/wiki/Ethernet "Ethernet") connection between the two HDMI connected devices so they can share an Internet connection,[[39]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteHEC-39) introduced an audio return channel (ARC),[[37]](#cite_note-HDMIwebsiteARC-37) 3D Over HDMI, a new Micro HDMI Connector, an expanded set of color spaces with the addition of sYCC601, [Adobe RGB](/wiki/Adobe_RGB "Adobe RGB") and Adobe YCC601, and an Automotive Connection System.[[62]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14PressRelease-62)[[96]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14Introduction-96)[[97]](#cite_note-IntroducingHDMIVersion143DPage-97)[[98]](#cite_note-IntroducingHDMIVersion144KSupportPage-98)[[99]](#cite_note-HDMI14SpecificationFeatures-99) HDMI 1.4 defined several [stereoscopic 3D](/wiki/Stereoscopic_3D "Stereoscopic 3D") formats including field alternative (interlaced), frame packing (a full resolution top-bottom format), line alternative full, side-by-side half, side-by-side full, [2D + depth](/wiki/2D_plus_depth "2D plus depth"), and 2D + depth + graphics + graphics depth ([WOWvx](/wiki/WOWvx "WOWvx")).[[61]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14FAQ-61)[[100]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14LaunchDocument-100)[[101]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14UpdateDocument-101) HDMI 1.4 requires that 3D displays implement the frame packing 3D format at either 720p50 and 1080p24 or 720p60 and 1080p24.[[101]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14UpdateDocument-101) High Speed HDMI cables as defined in HDMI 1.3 work with all HDMI 1.4 features except for the HDMI Ethernet Channel, which requires the new High Speed HDMI Cable with Ethernet defined in HDMI 1.4.[[61]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14FAQ-61)[[100]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14LaunchDocument-100)[[101]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14UpdateDocument-101)

**HDMI 1.4a** was released on March 4, 2010, and added two mandatory 3D formats for broadcast content, which was deferred with HDMI 1.4 pending the direction of the 3D broadcast market.[[102]](#cite_note-hdmi_1.4a_pr-102)[[103]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14a20100305-103) HDMI 1.4a has defined mandatory 3D formats for broadcast, game, and movie content.[[102]](#cite_note-hdmi_1.4a_pr-102) HDMI 1.4a requires that 3D displays implement the frame packing 3D format at either 720p50 and 1080p24 or 720p60 and 1080p24, side-by-side horizontal at either 1080i50 or 1080i60, and top-and-bottom at either 720p50 and 1080p24 or 720p60 and 1080p24.[[103]](#cite_note-HDMIVersion14a20100305-103)

**HDMI 1.4b** was released on October 11, 2011,[[104]](#cite_note-HDMIAnnouncement20111011-104) containing only minor clarifications to the 1.4a document. HDMI 1.4b is the last version of the standard that HDMI LA is responsible for. All later versions of the HDMI Specification are produced by the HDMI Forum, created on October 25, 2011.[[28]](#cite_note-HDMIPressRelease20111025-28)[[105]](#cite_note-HDMIForumFAQVersion-105)

### Version 2.0

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Version 2.0")]

HDMI 2.0, referred to by some manufacturers as HDMI [UHD](/wiki/Ultra-high-definition_television "Ultra-high-definition television"), was released on September 4, 2013.[[106]](#cite_note-hdmi_2.0_pr-106)

HDMI 2.0 increases the maximum bandwidth to 18.0 Gbit/s.[[106]](#cite_note-hdmi_2.0_pr-106)[[107]](#cite_note-HDMISpecification2.0-107)[[108]](#cite_note-HDMI2.0FAQ-108) HDMI 2.0 uses TMDS encoding for video transmission like previous versions, giving it a maximum video bandwidth of 14.4 Gbit/s. This enables HDMI 2.0 to carry 4K video at 60 Hz with 24 bit/px color depth.[[106]](#cite_note-hdmi_2.0_pr-106)[[109]](#cite_note-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110) Other features of HDMI 2.0 include support for the [Rec. 2020](/wiki/Rec._2020 "Rec. 2020") color space, up to 32 audio channels, up to 1536 kHz audio sample frequency, dual video streams to multiple users on the same screen, up to four audio streams, 4:2:0 chroma subsampling, 25 fps 3D formats, support for the 21:9 aspect ratio, dynamic synchronization of video and audio streams, the [HE-AAC](/wiki/HE-AAC "HE-AAC") and [DRA](/wiki/Dynamic_Resolution_Adaptation "Dynamic Resolution Adaptation") audio standards, improved 3D capability, and additional CEC functions.[[106]](#cite_note-hdmi_2.0_pr-106)[[111]](#cite_note-HDMI2IntroductionSeptember2013-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)

**HDMI 2.0a** was released on April 8, 2015, and added support for [High Dynamic Range (HDR) video](/wiki/High-dynamic-range_video "High-dynamic-range video") with static metadata.[[113]](#cite_note-HDMI20aReleasedTwice-113)

**HDMI 2.0b** was released March 2016.[[114]](#cite_note-114) HDMI 2.0b initially supported the same [HDR10](/wiki/HDR10 "HDR10") standard as HDMI 2.0a as specified in the CTA-861.3 specification.[[111]](#cite_note-HDMI2IntroductionSeptember2013-111) In December 2016 additional support for HDR Video transport was added to HDMI 2.0b in the CTA-861-G specification, which extends the static metadata signaling to include [hybrid log–gamma](/wiki/Hybrid_log%E2%80%93gamma "Hybrid log–gamma") (HLG).[[111]](#cite_note-HDMI2IntroductionSeptember2013-111)[[115]](#cite_note-HLGHDMI2016Flatpanels-115)[[116]](#cite_note-HLGHDMI2016BBC-116)

### Version 2.1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Version 2.1")]

HDMI 2.1 was officially announced by the HDMI Forum on January 4, 2017,[[117]](#cite_note-HDMI21Announcement-117)[[118]](#cite_note-HDMI21Introduction-118) and was released on November 28, 2017.[[119]](#cite_note-HDMI21Release-119) It adds support for higher resolutions and higher refresh rates, including 4K 120 Hz and [8K](/wiki/8K_resolution "8K resolution") 60 Hz. HDMI 2.1 also introduces a new HDMI cable category called *Ultra High Speed* (referred to as *48G* during development), which certifies cables at the new higher speeds that these formats require. Ultra High Speed HDMI cables are backwards compatible with older HDMI devices, and older cables are compatible with new HDMI 2.1 devices, though the full 48 Gbit/s bandwidth is only supported with the new cables.

Some systems may not be able to use HDMI 2.1 because the HDMI Forum is preventing its use in open source implementations (such as Linux open source drivers). Users of those systems may need to use [DisplayPort](/wiki/DisplayPort "DisplayPort") instead to access high resolutions and speeds.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

The following features were added to the HDMI 2.1 Specification:[[119]](#cite_note-HDMI21Release-119)[[121]](#cite_note-HDMI21January2017Anandtech-121)

* Maximum supported format is [10K](/wiki/10K_resolution "10K resolution") at 120 Hz
* Dynamic HDR for specifying HDR metadata on a scene-by-scene or even a frame-by-frame basis
  + Note: While HDMI 2.1 did standardize transport of dynamic HDR metadata over HDMI, in actuality it only formalized dynamic metadata interfaces already utilized by Dolby Vision and [HDR10+](/wiki/HDR10%2B "HDR10+") in HDMI 2.0, which is why neither Dolby Vision nor HDR10+ require HDMI 2.1 to function properly.[[122]](#cite_note-122)
* [Display Stream Compression](/wiki/Display_Stream_Compression "Display Stream Compression") (DSC) 1.2 is used for video formats higher than 8K with 4:2:0 chroma subsampling
* [High Frame Rate](/wiki/High_Frame_Rate "High Frame Rate") (HFR) for 4K, 8K, and 10K, which adds support for refresh rates up to 120 Hz
* Enhanced Audio Return Channel (eARC) for object-based audio formats such as [Dolby Atmos](/wiki/Dolby_Atmos "Dolby Atmos") and [DTS:X](/wiki/DTS:X "DTS:X")
* Enhanced refresh rate and latency reduction features:
  + Variable Refresh Rate (VRR) reduces or eliminates lag, stutter and frame tearing for more fluid motion in games
  + Quick Media Switching (QMS) for movies and video eliminates the delay that can result in blank screens before content begins to be displayed
  + Quick Frame Transport (QFT) reduces latency by bursting individual pictures across the HDMI link as fast as possible when the link's hardware supports more bandwidth than the minimum amount needed for the resolution and frame rate of the content. With QFT, individual pictures arrive earlier and some hardware blocks can be fully powered off for longer periods of time between pictures to reduce heat generation and extend battery life.
* Auto Low Latency Mode (ALLM) – When a display device supports the option to either optimize its pixel processing for best latency or best pixel processing, ALLM allows the current HDMI source device to automatically select, based on its better understanding of the nature of its own content, which mode the user would most likely prefer.

Video formats that require more bandwidth than 18.0 Gbit/s (4K 60 Hz 8 bpc RGB), such as 4K 60 Hz 10 bpc (HDR), 4K 120 Hz, and 8K 60 Hz, may require the new "Ultra High Speed" or "Ultra High Speed with Ethernet" cables.[[118]](#cite_note-HDMI21Introduction-118) HDMI 2.1's other new features are supported with existing HDMI cables.

The increase in maximum bandwidth is achieved by increasing both the bitrate of the data channels and the number of channels. Previous HDMI versions use three data channels (each operating at up to 6.0 Gbit/s in HDMI 2.0, or up to 3.4 Gbit/s in HDMI 1.4), with an additional channel for the TMDS clock signal, which runs at a fraction of the data channel speed (one tenth the speed, or up to 340 MHz, for signaling rates up to 3.4 Gbit/s; one fortieth the speed, or up to 150 MHz, for signaling rates between 3.4 and 6.0 Gbit/s). HDMI 2.1 doubles the signaling rate of the data channels to 12 Gbit/s. The structure of the data has been changed to use a new packet-based format with an embedded clock signal, which allows what was formerly the TMDS clock channel to be used as a fourth data channel instead, increasing the signaling rate across that channel to 12 Gbit/s as well. These changes increase the aggregate bandwidth from 18.0 Gbit/s (3 × 6.0 Gbit/s) to 48.0 Gbit/s (4 × 12.0 Gbit/s), a 2.66× improvement in bandwidth. In addition, the data is transmitted more efficiently by using a 16b/18b encoding scheme, which uses a larger percentage of the bandwidth for data rather than DC balancing compared to the TMDS scheme used by previous versions (88.8% compared to 80%). This, in combination with the 2.66× bandwidth, raises the maximum data rate of HDMI 2.1 from 14.4 Gbit/s to 42.6 Gbit/s. Subtracting overhead for FEC, the usable data rate is approximately 42.0 Gbit/s, around 2.92× the data rate of HDMI 2.0.[[123]](#cite_note-HDTVMagazine_HDMI2.1-123)[[124]](#cite_note-HDGuru_HDMI2.1-124)

The 48 Gbit/s bandwidth provided by HDMI 2.1 is enough for 8K resolution at approximately 50 Hz, with 8 bpc RGB or Y′CBCR 4:4:4 color. To achieve even higher formats, HDMI 2.1 can use [Display Stream Compression](/wiki/Display_Stream_Compression "Display Stream Compression") (DSC) with a compression ratio of up to 3∶1. Using DSC, formats up to 8K (7680 × 4320) 120 Hz or 10K (10240 × 4320) 100 Hz at 8 bpc RGB/4:4:4 are possible. Using Y′CBCR with 4:2:2 or 4:2:0 chroma subsampling in combination with DSC can allow for even higher formats.[[121]](#cite_note-HDMI21January2017Anandtech-121)

**HDMI 2.1a** was released on February 15, 2022, and added support for Source-Based Tone Mapping (SBTM).[[125]](#cite_note-hdmi_2.1a_pr-125)[[126]](#cite_note-tftcentral-sbtm-126)

**HDMI 2.1b** was released on August 10, 2023.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

### Version 2.2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Version 2.2")]

**HDMI 2.2** was announced on January 6, 2025, it was released on June 25, 2025.[[128]](#cite_note-128) The maximum allowed bit rate is increased to 96 Gbit/s and Latency Indication Protocol (LIP) support is added for improving [audio and video synchronization](/wiki/Audio-to-video_synchronization "Audio-to-video synchronization").[[129]](#cite_note-129)

1. **[^](#cite_ref-bandwidth_138-0)** Total transmission bit rate is equal to the number of data channels multiplied by the bit rate per channel (binary digits transmitted per second). Each channel transmits one bit (binary digit) per signal, and signals at ten times the character rate. Therefore, the total transmission bit rate (in Mbit/s) = 10 bits × (character rate in MHz) × (# of data channels).
2. **[^](#cite_ref-data_rate_139-0)** Some of the transmitted bits are used for encoding purposes rather than representing data, so the rate at which video data can be transmitted across the HDMI interface is only a portion of the total bit rate.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-char_rate_140-0)** The TMDS character rate is the number of 10-bit TMDS characters per second transmitted across one HDMI data channel. This is sometimes informally referred to as the pixel clock or TMDS clock because these terms were once equivalent in past HDMI versions.[[107]](#cite_note-HDMISpecification2.0-107): §4.2.2
4. **[^](#cite_ref-encoding_143-0)** TMDS encoding uses 10 bits of the transmission to send 8 bits of data, so only 80% of the transmission bit rate is available for data throughput. 16b/18b encoding uses 18 bits of bandwidth to send 16 bits of data, so 88.8% of the transmission bit rate is available for data throughput.

## Resolution and refresh frequency limits

### Refresh frequency limits for common resolutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Refresh frequency limits for common resolutions")]

The maximum limits for TMDS transmission are calculated using standard data rate calculations.[[144]](#cite_note-152) For FRL transmission, the limits are calculated using the capacity computation algorithm provided by the HDMI Specification.[[145]](#cite_note-HDMI_2.1a-153): §6.5.6.2.1 All calculations assume uncompressed RGB video with [CVT-RB v2](/wiki/Coordinated_Video_Timings "Coordinated Video Timings") timing. Maximum limits may differ if compression (i.e. DSC) or Y′CBCR 4:2:0 chroma subsampling are used.

Display manufacturers may also use non-standard blanking intervals (a Vendor-Specific Timing Format as defined in the HDMI Specification[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §6.1) rather than CVT-RB v2 to achieve even higher frequencies when bandwidth is a constraint. The refresh frequencies in the below table do not represent the absolute maximum limit of each interface, but rather an estimate based on a modern standardized timing formula. The minimum blanking intervals (and therefore the exact maximum frequency that can be achieved) will depend on the display and how many secondary data packets it requires, and therefore will differ from model to model.

### Refresh frequency limits for standard video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Refresh frequency limits for standard video")]

HDMI 1.0 and 1.1 are restricted to transmitting only certain video formats,[[88]](#cite_note-HDMI1.0-88): §6.1 defined in EIA/CEA-861-B and in the HDMI Specification itself.[[88]](#cite_note-HDMI1.0-88): §6.3 HDMI 1.2 and all later versions allow any arbitrary resolution and frame rate (within the bandwidth limit). Formats that are not supported by the HDMI Specification (i.e., no standardized timings defined) may be implemented as a vendor-specific format. Successive versions of the HDMI Specification continue to add support for additional formats (such as 4K resolutions), but the added support is to establish standardized timings to ensure interoperability between products, not to establish which formats are or are not permitted. Video formats do not require explicit support from the HDMI Specification in order to be transmitted and displayed.[[89]](#cite_note-HDMI1.2a-89): §6.1

Individual products may have heavier limitations than those listed below, since HDMI devices are not required to support the maximum bandwidth of the HDMI version that they implement. Therefore, it is not guaranteed that a display will support the refresh rates listed in this table, even if the display has the required HDMI version.

### Feature support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Feature support")]

The features defined in the HDMI specification that an HDMI device may implement are listed below. For historical interest, the version of the HDMI specification in which the feature was first added is also listed. All features of the HDMI specification are optional; HDMI devices may implement any combination of these features.

Although the "HDMI version numbers" are commonly misused as a way of indicating that a device supports certain features, this notation has no official meaning and is considered improper by HDMI Licensing.[[146]](#cite_note-167) There is no officially defined correlation between features supported by a device and any claimed "version numbers", as version numbers refer to historical editions of the HDMI specification document, not to particular classes of HDMI devices. Manufacturers are forbidden from describing their devices using HDMI version numbers, and are required to identify support for features by listing explicit support for them,[[147]](#cite_note-168)[[148]](#cite_note-169) but the HDMI forum has received criticism for lack of enforcement of these policies.[[149]](#cite_note-170)

* Full HD [Blu-ray Disc](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc") and [HD DVD](/wiki/HD_DVD "HD DVD") video (version 1.0)[[a]](#cite_note-feature-support-audio-codecs-171)
* Consumer Electronic Control (CEC) (version 1.0)[[b]](#cite_note-174)
* [DVD-Audio](/wiki/DVD-Audio "DVD-Audio") (version 1.1)[[a]](#cite_note-feature-support-audio-codecs-171)
* [Super Audio CD](/wiki/Super_Audio_CD "Super Audio CD") ([DSD](/wiki/Direct_Stream_Digital "Direct Stream Digital")) (version 1.2)
* Auto [Lip-Sync](/wiki/Audio-to-video_synchronization "Audio-to-video synchronization") Correction (version 1.3)
* [Dolby TrueHD](/wiki/Dolby_TrueHD "Dolby TrueHD") / [DTS-HD Master Audio](/wiki/DTS-HD_Master_Audio "DTS-HD Master Audio") bitstream capable (version 1.3)
* Updated list of CEC commands (version 1.3a)[[c]](#cite_note-175)
* 3D video (version 1.4)[[97]](#cite_note-IntroducingHDMIVersion143DPage-97)
* Ethernet channel (100 Mbit/s) (version 1.4)
* Audio return channel (ARC) (version 1.4)
* 4 audio streams (version 2.0)[[111]](#cite_note-HDMI2IntroductionSeptember2013-111)
* Dual View (version 2.0)[[111]](#cite_note-HDMI2IntroductionSeptember2013-111)
* [Perceptual quantizer](/wiki/Perceptual_quantizer "Perceptual quantizer") HDR EOTF (SMPTE ST 2084) (version 2.0a)[[152]](#cite_note-HDMI20bfaq-176)

### Display Stream Compression

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Display Stream Compression")]

*[Display Stream Compression](/wiki/Display_Stream_Compression "Display Stream Compression")* (DSC) is a [VESA](/wiki/VESA "VESA")-developed video compression algorithm designed to enable increased display resolutions and frame rates over existing physical interfaces, and make devices smaller and lighter, with longer battery life.[[153]](#cite_note-DSC-177)

### Blu-ray Disc and HD DVD players

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Blu-ray Disc and HD DVD players")]

[Blu-ray Disc](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc "Blu-ray Disc") and [HD DVD](/wiki/HD_DVD "HD DVD"), introduced in 2006, offer high-fidelity audio features that require HDMI for best results. HDMI 1.3 can transport [Dolby Digital Plus](/wiki/Dolby_Digital_Plus "Dolby Digital Plus"), [Dolby TrueHD](/wiki/Dolby_TrueHD "Dolby TrueHD"), and [DTS-HD Master Audio](/wiki/DTS-HD_Master_Audio "DTS-HD Master Audio") bitstreams in compressed form.[[33]](#cite_note-HDMI1.3a-33): §7 This capability allows for an [AV receiver](/wiki/AV_receiver "AV receiver") with the necessary decoder to decode the compressed audio stream. The Blu-ray specification does not include video encoded with either deep color or xvYCC; thus, HDMI 1.0 can transfer Blu-ray discs at full video quality.[[154]](#cite_note-178)

The HDMI 1.4 specification (released in 2009) added support for 3D video and is used by all Blu-ray 3D compatible players.

The Blu-ray Disc Association (BDA) spokespersons have stated (Sept. 2014 at IFA show in Berlin, Germany) that the Blu-ray, Ultra HD players, and 4K discs are expected to be available starting in the second half to 2015. It is anticipated that such Blu-ray UHD players will be required to include a HDMI 2.0 output that supports HDCP 2.2.

Blu-ray permits secondary audio decoding, whereby the disc content can tell the player to mix multiple audio sources together before final output.[[155]](#cite_note-179) Some Blu-ray and HD DVD players can decode all of the audio [codecs](/wiki/Codec "Codec") internally and can output LPCM audio over HDMI. Multichannel LPCM can be transported over an HDMI connection, and as long as the [AV receiver](/wiki/AV_receiver "AV receiver") implements multichannel LPCM audio over HDMI and implements [HDCP](/wiki/HDCP "HDCP"), the audio reproduction is equal in resolution to HDMI 1.3 bitstream output. Some low-cost AV receivers, such as the Onkyo TX-SR506, do not allow audio processing over HDMI and are labelled as "HDMI pass through" devices.[[156]](#cite_note-180)[[157]](#cite_note-181) Virtually all modern AV Receivers now offer HDMI 1.4 inputs and outputs with processing for all of the audio formats offered by Blu-ray Discs and other HD video sources. During 2014 several manufacturers introduced premium AV Receivers that include one, or multiple, HDMI 2.0 inputs along with a HDMI 2.0 output(s). However, not until 2015 did most major manufacturers of AV receivers also support HDCP 2.2 as needed to support certain high quality UHD video sources, such as Blu-ray UHD players.

### Digital cameras and camcorders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=HDMI&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Digital cameras and camcorders")]

Most consumer camcorders, as well as many digital cameras, are equipped with a mini-HDMI connector (type C connector).

Some cameras also have 4K capability,
although cameras capable of [HD video](/wiki/HD_video "HD video") often include an HDMI interface for playback or even [live preview](/wiki/Live_preview "Live preview"), the [image processor](/wiki/Image_processor "Image processor") and the [video processor](/wiki/Video_processor "Video processor") of cameras usable for uncompressed video must be able to deliver the full [image resolution](/wiki/Image_resolution "Image resolution") at the specified [frame rate](/wiki/Frame_rate "Frame rate") in [real time](/wiki/Real-time_computing#Real-time_in_Digital_Signal_Processing "Real-time computing") without any missing frames causing jitter. In addition, to be used as a video source, such as for streaming or switching, the camera must also be capable of removing the UI elements from the image. Therefore, usable uncompressed video out of HDMI without the device's UI elements is often called "clean HDMI".[[158]](#cite_note-182)[[159]](#cite_note-183)
